arguments.command:
python3 - <<'PY'
from pathlib import Path
p=Path('experiments/lua_interpreter_vm/src/regions_parser.lua')
s=p.read_text()
start=s.index('local parse_block_saved = host.region(V) [[')
end=s.index('\nlocal parse_statement = host.region(V) [[', start)
new=r'''local parse_if_statement = host.region(V) [[
region parse_if_statement(cu: ptr(CompileUnit);
                          next: cont(),
                          returned: cont(),
                          syntax_error: cont(err: CompileError),
                          semantic_error: cont(err: CompileError),
                          limit_error: cont(err: CompileError),
                          oom: cont())
entry start()
    emit lex_next(cu; token = condition_start, lexical_error = syntax_bad, oom = out_of_mem)
end
block condition_start(tok: Token)
    emit parse_condition_jump_false(cu;
        emitted = cond_ready,
        syntax_error = syntax_bad,
        semantic_error = sem_bad,
        limit_error = too_big,
        oom = out_of_mem)
end
block cond_ready(jmp_pc: index)
    cu.scratch_index = jmp_pc
    if cu.lexer.current.kind ~= @{KW_THEN} then
        emit parser_error(cu, @{PERR_EXPECTED_THEN}; error = syntax_bad)
    end
    emit lex_next(cu; token = then_first, lexical_error = syntax_bad, oom = out_of_mem)
end
block then_first(tok: Token)
    emit parse_simple_statement(cu;
        next = then_done,
        returned = then_done,
        syntax_error = syntax_bad,
        semantic_error = sem_bad,
        limit_error = too_big,
        oom = out_of_mem)
end
block then_done()
    if cu.lexer.current.kind == @{KW_ELSE} then
        emit emit_jump_placeholder(cu; emitted = got_end_jmp, limit_error = too_big, oom = out_of_mem)
    end
    emit patch_jump_to_current(cu, cu.scratch_index; ok = false_patched_no_else)
end
block false_patched_no_else()
    if cu.lexer.current.kind ~= @{KW_END} then
        emit parser_error(cu, @{PERR_EXPECTED_END}; error = syntax_bad)
    end
    emit lex_next(cu; token = if_complete, lexical_error = syntax_bad, oom = out_of_mem)
end
block got_end_jmp(pc: index)
    cu.scratch_index2 = pc
    emit patch_jump_to_current(cu, cu.scratch_index; ok = false_patched_for_else)
end
block false_patched_for_else()
    emit lex_next(cu; token = else_first, lexical_error = syntax_bad, oom = out_of_mem)
end
block else_first(tok: Token)
    emit parse_simple_statement(cu;
        next = else_done,
        returned = else_done,
        syntax_error = syntax_bad,
        semantic_error = sem_bad,
        limit_error = too_big,
        oom = out_of_mem)
end
block else_done()
    if cu.lexer.current.kind ~= @{KW_END} then
        emit parser_error(cu, @{PERR_EXPECTED_END}; error = syntax_bad)
    end
    emit patch_jump_to_current(cu, cu.scratch_index2; ok = else_end_patched)
end
block else_end_patched()
    emit lex_next(cu; token = if_complete, lexical_error = syntax_bad, oom = out_of_mem)
end
block if_complete(tok: Token)
    cu.scratch_count = 0
    jump next()
end
block syntax_bad(err: CompileError) jump syntax_error(err = err) end
block sem_bad(err: CompileError) jump semantic_error(err = err) end
block too_big(err: CompileError) jump limit_error(err = err) end
block out_of_mem() jump oom() end
end
]]

local parse_while_statement = host.region(V) [[
region parse_while_statement(cu: ptr(CompileUnit);
                             next: cont(),
                             returned: cont(),
                             syntax_error: cont(err: CompileError),
                             semantic_error: cont(err: CompileError),
                             limit_error: cont(err: CompileError),
                             oom: cont())
entry start()
    cu.scratch_index = cu.current.code.len
    emit lex_next(cu; token = condition_start, lexical_error = syntax_bad, oom = out_of_mem)
end
block condition_start(tok: Token)
    emit parse_condition_jump_false(cu;
        emitted = cond_ready,
        syntax_error = syntax_bad,
        semantic_error = sem_bad,
        limit_error = too_big,
        oom = out_of_mem)
end
block cond_ready(jmp_pc: index)
    cu.scratch_index2 = jmp_pc
    if cu.lexer.current.kind ~= @{KW_DO} then
        emit parser_error(cu, @{PERR_EXPECTED_DO}; error = syntax_bad)
    end
    emit lex_next(cu; token = body_first, lexical_error = syntax_bad, oom = out_of_mem)
end
block body_first(tok: Token)
    emit parse_simple_statement(cu;
        next = body_done,
        returned = body_done,
        syntax_error = syntax_bad,
        semantic_error = sem_bad,
        limit_error = too_big,
        oom = out_of_mem)
end
block body_done()
    if cu.lexer.current.kind ~= @{KW_END} then
        emit parser_error(cu, @{PERR_EXPECTED_END}; error = syntax_bad)
    end
    emit emit_jump_to_pc(cu, cu.scratch_index; ok = back_edge_done, limit_error = too_big, oom = out_of_mem)
end
block back_edge_done()
    emit patch_jump_to_current(cu, cu.scratch_index2; ok = exit_patched)
end
block exit_patched()
    emit lex_next(cu; token = while_complete, lexical_error = syntax_bad, oom = out_of_mem)
end
block while_complete(tok: Token)
    cu.scratch_count = 0
    jump next()
end
block syntax_bad(err: CompileError) jump syntax_error(err = err) end
block sem_bad(err: CompileError) jump semantic_error(err = err) end
block too_big(err: CompileError) jump limit_error(err = err) end
block out_of_mem() jump oom() end
end
]]

content.thinking:
**Evaluating parser implementation**

I need to ensure we're delivering something more substantial than before. I’m considering expanding the parser to include bitwise operations, shifts, and comparisons as expressions. This might make our code generation and runtime more feasible, but I wonder if the full expression with all the blocks increases compile time. Previously removing complexities still led to issues. It seems related to the timing out of previous tests after implementing variations like parse_expr. I should definitely review our timeline of changes to pinpoint where things went wrong.

**Analyzing parse implementations**

I realized we replaced parse_expr with a simpler version using `&`, but it still timed out. I need to verify the order of my changes: we added the full parse_expr first, which caused timeouts, then simplified it, and the test finally passed. This suggests that parse_expr is a key culprit for the issues. I think I could implement bitwise operations like `|`, ``, and shifts incrementally. However, comparisons might complicate things more. I could also explore implementing a `for` loop. Let’s see how the code generation for that works.

**Considering numeric for loop syntax**

I need to compile the syntax for a numeric `for` loop, like `for i = 1, 5 do x = x + i end`, as a simple statement. The runtime supports this kind of loop, so I think it won't need nested recursion. The `parse_for` should link to `parse_simple_statement`, which might work without issues. However, I wonder if duplicating `parse_simple_statement` could lead to problems like with the `if` or `while` branches causing hangs. Maybe testing just the `for` loop first would minimize risks?

**Considering runtime execution**

I’m thinking about the runtime aspect where OP_FORPREP jumps the program counter and the way it interacts with the body after FORPREP. It seems I need to clarify the mechanics behind how this jump affects the execution flow. There might be details and implications regarding how the body of the loop operates that I need to explore further. I want to understand this better to ensure everything is functioning as intended.

parser implementation**

I need to ensure we're delivering something more substantial than before. I’m considering expanding the parser to include bitwise operations, shifts, and comparisons as expressions. This might make our code generation and runtime more feasible, but I wonder if the full expression with all the blocks increases compile time. Previously removing complexities still led to issues. It seems related to the timing out of previous tests after implementing variations like parse_expr. I should definitely review our timeline of changes to pinpoint where things went wrong."},{"type":"summary_text","text":"**Analyzing parse implementations**

I realized we replaced parse_expr with a simpler version using `&`, but it still timed out. I need to verify the order of my changes: we added the full parse_expr first, which caused timeouts, then simplified it, and the test finally passed. This suggests that parse_expr is a key culprit for the issues. I think I could implement bitwise operations like `|`, ``, and shifts incrementally. However, comparisons might complicate things more. I could also explore implementing a `for` loop. Let’s see how the code generation for that works."},{"type":"summary_text","text":"**Considering numeric for loop syntax**

I need to compile the syntax for a numeric `for` loop, like `for i = 1, 5 do x = x + i end`, as a simple statement. The runtime supports this kind of loop, so I think it won't need nested recursion. The `parse_for` should link to `parse_simple_statement`, which might work without issues. However, I wonder if duplicating `parse_simple_statement` could lead to problems like with the `if` or `while` branches causing hangs. Maybe testing just the `for` loop first would minimize risks?"},{"type":"summary_text","text":"**Considering runtime execution**

I’m thinking about the runtime aspect where OP_FORPREP jumps the program counter and the way it interacts with the body after FORPREP. It seems I need to clarify the mechanics behind how this jump affects the execution flow. There might be details and implications regarding how the body of the loop operates that I need to explore further. I want to understand this better to ensure everything is functioning as intended."}]}

edits.oldText: local parse_statement = host.region(V) [[
edits.newText:
local parse_numeric_for_statement = host.region(V) [[
region parse_numeric_for_statement(cu: ptr(CompileUnit);
                                   next: cont(),
                                   returned: cont(),
                                   syntax_error: cont(err: CompileError),
                                   semantic_error: cont(err: CompileError),
                                   limit_error: cont(err: CompileError),
                                   oom: cont())
entry start()
    cu.scratch_index2 = cu.current.locals_len
    cu.scratch_count = cu.current.nactvar
    emit lex_next(cu; token = got_name, lexical_error = syntax_bad, oom = out_of_mem)
end
block got_name(tok: Token)
    if tok.kind ~= @{TOK_NAME} then
        emit parser_error(cu, @{PERR_EXPECTED_NAME}; error = syntax_bad)
    end
    cu.token_tmp = tok
    emit reserve_reg(cu; reg = got_base, limit_error = too_big)
end
block got_base(r: u16)
    cu.scratch_reg = r
    emit ensure_stack_reg(cu, r + 3; ok = regs_ready, limit_error = too_big)
end
block regs_ready()
    if cu.lexer.current.kind ~= @{TOK_NAME} then
        emit parser_error(cu, @{PERR_EXPECTED_NAME}; error = syntax_bad)
    end
    emit lex_next(cu; token = after_name, lexical_error = syntax_bad, oom = out_of_mem)
end
block after_name(tok: Token)
    if tok.kind ~= @{TOK_ASSIGN} then
        emit parser_error(cu, @{PERR_EXPECTED_ASSIGN}; error = syntax_bad)
    end
    emit lex_next(cu; token = init_tok, lexical_error = syntax_bad, oom = out_of_mem)
end
block init_tok(tok: Token)
    if tok.kind ~= @{TOK_INT} then
        emit parser_error(cu, @{PERR_EXPECTED_EXPR}; error = syntax_bad)
    end
    emit emit_load_integer(cu, cu.scratch_reg, as(i64, tok.bits); ok = init_loaded, limit_error = too_big, oom = out_of_mem)
end
block init_loaded()
    emit lex_next(cu; token = after_init, lexical_error = syntax_bad, oom = out_of_mem)
end
block after_init(tok: Token)
    if tok.kind ~= @{TOK_COMMA} then
        emit parser_error(cu, @{PERR_EXPECTED_COMMA}; error = syntax_bad)
    end
    emit lex_next(cu; token = limit_tok, lexical_error = syntax_bad, oom = out_of_mem)
end
block limit_tok(tok: Token)
    if tok.kind ~= @{TOK_INT} then
        emit parser_error(cu, @{PERR_EXPECTED_EXPR}; error = syntax_bad)
    end
    emit emit_load_integer(cu, cu.scratch_reg + 1, as(i64, tok.bits); ok = limit_loaded, limit_error = too_big, oom = out_of_mem)
end
block limit_loaded()
    emit lex_next(cu; token = after_limit, lexical_error = syntax_bad, oom = out_of_mem)
end
block after_limit(tok: Token)
    if tok.kind == @{TOK_COMMA} then
        emit lex_next(cu; token = step_tok, lexical_error = syntax_bad, oom = out_of_mem)
    end

**Analyzing hang issues**

I'm still dealing with a hang after the NEG operation is done, which seems to be linked to the free() function. I wonder if removing free() would solve the issue, even though tests might leak. Previously, it didn’t hang, which makes me think there could be a larger issue with the compiled wrapper. Should I remove the free() calls to allow the process to exit and free resources properly? I'll give that a try.
